sections.context: The Todos API (app.js, Express) currently has no request logging at all — grep for pino/logger/durationMs/requestId returns nothing, and package.json only depends on express. Coord coord-ac249ad6 requires that every API request emits exactly one structured JSON log line via Pino, carrying method, path, status, durationMs, and requestId, with a unique requestId per request and no body/header logging. This is needed for operational visibility (one auditable line per request) and to establish the requestId convention for future cross-cutting concerns.
sections.approach: Add a single Pino-backed request-logging middleware mounted at the top of the Express stack in app.js, right after express.json() and before any route. The middleware will: (1) assign a requestId per request using crypto.randomUUID() (unique per request, no external dependency, no header requirement), attaching it to req.id; (2) start a high-resolution timer (process.hrtime.bigint or Date.now) on request entry; (3) log exactly once on res.on('finish') using pino, including method (req.method), path (req.path/req.url minus query), status (res.statusCode), durationMs (computed from the timer), and requestId (req.id). Exactly-one-line is guaranteed because 'finish' fires exactly once per request. pino is added to package.json dependencies. The logger instance is exported so tests can capture its output. We deliberately avoid pino-http/auto-request-logging to keep full control of the one-line invariant and the exact fields, and we do not log bodies or headers per the non-goals.

alternatives.name: Use pino-http middleware
alternatives.rejected_because: It logs on its own hooks and adds extra/autogenerated fields; harder to guarantee exactly one line and the exact field set, and risks duplicate lines if combined with a custom handler. A hand-rolled finish-handler gives precise control over the one-line invariant and field set.

alternatives.name: Use morgan with JSON format
alternatives.rejected_because: Violates the explicit constraint to use Pino for logging.

alternatives.name: Read requestId from an inbound header (e.g. x-request-id)
alternatives.rejected_because: The success criterion requires each request to get a unique requestId with no header dependency or trust assumptions; crypto.randomUUID() guarantees uniqueness without header input and stays within non-goals (we are not logging headers, only generating an id).

steps.title: Add Pino request-logging middleware to app.js
steps.description: Add pino to package.json dependencies and install it. Create/export a Pino logger instance and mount a request-logging middleware in app.js after express.json() and before the routes. The middleware must assign a unique requestId via crypto.randomUUID() to req.id, start a timer on entry, and emit exactly ONE log line on res.on('finish') containing method, path (route path without query string), status, durationMs, and requestId. Must not log request/response bodies or headers. Ensure the finish handler is idempotent so exactly one line is emitted even under error/aborted paths. Export the logger so tests can capture output.

steps.title: Add node --test coverage for request logging behavior
steps.description: Add a test file run via `node --test` (npm test already maps to it) that exercises the existing routes (GET /todos, POST /todos, DELETE /todos/:id incl. 400/404) and asserts: (1) each request produces exactly one valid JSON log line; (2) each line contains all required fields (method, path, status, durationMs, requestId) with correct values; (3) requestId differs per request (uniqueness). Capture Pino output via a custom writable destination stream injected through the exported logger (e.g. a logger factory that accepts a stream), so no extra test dependencies (no supertest) are required. Assert the no-body/no-header non-goal holds (line keys contain only the allowed fields).

risks: res.on('finish') must fire exactly once — guard against double-logging if any error middleware or abort path also triggers logging; add an emitted-flag guard.
durationMs must be non-negative and meaningful; use process.hrtime.bigint (or Date.now) and convert to integer ms.
path should be the route path without query string to avoid leaking query/body-adjacent data; strip query before logging.
If pino writes asynchronously to stdout by default, tests must capture output via a synchronous in-memory writable stream passed to the logger to avoid flaky timing.
Adding pino changes the dependency tree — ensure package-lock.json is updated so npm ci / install reproduces.
acceptance: Every API request (all routes incl. error responses) emits exactly one valid JSON log line via Pino.
Each log line contains method, path, status, durationMs, and requestId with correct values.
Each request receives a unique requestId (no two requests share one).
No request/response bodies and no headers appear in any log line.
`npm test` (node --test) passes and includes automated coverage verifying the one-line-per-request invariant, the field set, and requestId uniqueness.
Existing routes (GET/POST/DELETE /todos) behavior and status codes are unchanged.

name: Structured JSON request logging via Pino

sections.context: The Todos API (app.js, Express) currently has no request logging at all. Verified against the repo: package.json declares only an `express` dependency; grep for pino/logger/requestId/durationMs/req.id/crypto returns nothing; no test files exist yet; `npm test` is already wired to `node --test`; Node v22 is in use (crypto.randomUUID available globally). Coord coord-2297190f requires that every inbound request emit exactly ONE structured JSON log line via Pino carrying method, path, status code, durationMs, and requestId, with a unique requestId per request and no logging of bodies or headers. The four existing routes (GET /todos, POST /todos incl. 400 on bad input, DELETE /todos/:id incl. 400 invalid-id and 404 not-found) all need to be covered. This gives the service one auditable line per request and establishes the requestId convention for any future cross-cutting concerns.
sections.approach: Add a single Pino-backed request-logging middleware mounted in app.js right after express.json() and before the first route. The middleware will: (1) assign a unique requestId per request via crypto.randomUUID() (available in Node v22, no external dependency, no inbound-header requirement), attaching it to req.id; (2) capture a monotonic start time with process.hrtime.bigint() on request entry; (3) emit exactly ONE log line on res.on('finish') containing method (req.method), path (req.path — the route path without query string), status (res.statusCode), durationMs (integer milliseconds computed from the hrtime delta), and requestId (req.id). Exactly-one-line is guaranteed because res 'finish' fires exactly once per request; we additionally guard with an emitted flag so any double-trigger (e.g. error path also calling the handler) cannot produce a duplicate line. We add `pino` to package.json dependencies and run `npm install` so package-lock.json is updated. The Pino logger is built via an exported factory that accepts a destination stream, so the production logger writes to stdout while tests inject an in-memory writable stream to capture output synchronously (avoiding async-timing flakes). We deliberately avoid pino-http / automatic request logging and avoid morgan so we keep precise control of the one-line invariant and the exact field set, and we log nothing from req/res bodies or headers per the non-goals.

alternatives.name: Use pino-http middleware
alternatives.rejected_because: It logs on its own hooks and injects autogenerated fields; harder to guarantee the exactly-one-line invariant and the exact field set, and risks duplicate lines if combined with a custom handler. A hand-rolled finish-handler gives precise control.

alternatives.name: Use morgan with a JSON format
alternatives.rejected_because: Violates the explicit Coord constraint to use Pino as the logging library.

alternatives.name: Read requestId from an inbound header (e.g. x-request-id)
alternatives.rejected_because: The success criterion requires every request to receive a unique requestId with no header dependency or trust assumption. Generating crypto.randomUUID() guarantees uniqueness without requiring header input and stays within the non-goals (we only generate an id; we do not log headers).

steps.title: Add Pino request-logging middleware to app.js
steps.description: Add `pino` to package.json dependencies and run `npm install` so package-lock.json is updated. Create an exported Pino logger factory (accepts a destination stream, defaults to process.stdout) and mount a request-logging middleware in app.js immediately after `app.use(express.json())` and before any route. The middleware must: assign a unique requestId via crypto.randomUUID() to req.id; capture process.hrtime.bigint() on entry; emit exactly ONE log line on res.on('finish') (guarded by an emitted flag) containing method, path (req.path, no query string), status (res.statusCode), durationMs (integer ms from the hrtime delta), and requestId (req.id). Must NOT log request/response bodies or headers. Export the logger factory so tests can inject a destination. Existing routes (GET /todos, POST /todos, DELETE /todos/:id) and their status codes must remain unchanged.

steps.title: Add node --test coverage for request logging behavior
steps.description: Add a test file run via `node --test` (npm test already maps to it; no new test runner dependency — no supertest). Use Node's built-in http module (or express's app) to drive real requests through the middleware, and inject an in-memory writable destination stream into the exported Pino logger factory so output is captured synchronously. Cover all routes: GET /todos, POST /todos (success 201 and 400 on missing/blank title), DELETE /todos/:id (204 success, 400 invalid id, 404 not found). Assert per request: (1) exactly one valid JSON log line is emitted; (2) the line contains method, path, status, durationMs, and requestId with correct values (status matches the response status code; durationMs is a non-negative number); (3) requestId differs across requests (uniqueness). Also assert the non-goal holds: line keys contain only the allowed fields (no body or header data).

risks: res.on('finish') could in principle be paired with another code path that also logs; guard with an emitted flag so exactly one line is emitted even under error/abort paths.
durationMs must be a non-negative integer; compute from process.hrtime.bigint() and convert to ms to avoid float/zero values that look broken.
path must exclude the query string (use req.path) so query/body-adjacent data is never logged.
Pino writes to stdout asynchronously by default; tests must capture output via a synchronous in-memory writable stream passed to the logger to avoid flaky timing.
Adding pino changes the dependency tree — package-lock.json must be updated so npm ci/install reproduces.
A 204 response has no body and DELETE error cases (400/404) must still emit exactly one line — confirm the finish handler fires for all status codes.
acceptance: Every inbound request across all routes (GET /todos, POST /todos incl. 400, DELETE /todos/:id incl. 400 and 404) emits exactly one valid JSON log line via Pino.
Each log line contains method, path, status, durationMs, and requestId with correct values (status matches the response status code; durationMs is a non-negative number).
Every request receives a unique requestId — no two requests share one.
No request/response bodies and no headers appear in any log line.
`npm test` (node --test) passes and includes automated coverage verifying the one-line-per-request invariant, the field set, requestId uniqueness, and the no-body/no-header non-goal.
Existing route behavior and status codes are unchanged.

name: Structured JSON request logging via Pino
